note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old male presents with burning urination without fever, flank pain, or testicular pain. There are no reported systemic symptoms such as fever or chills. The patient has no known medication allergies. Pregnancy not applicable.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings are not assessed. No laboratory or imaging results available at this time.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis)  
  - Differential: Urethritis (possibly sexually transmitted), prostatitis (early or mild), non-infectious urethral irritation  
  - Red flags considered: Absence of fever, flank pain, or testicular pain reduces concern for pyelonephritis or complicated infection.

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage as outpatient with urgent care or primary care follow-up if symptoms worsen or fail to improve.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy appropriate for uncomplicated UTI in males, such as a 5-7 day course of nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, pending local resistance patterns and allergies. Recommend increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief with analgesics such as phenazopyridine if available. Advise patient to avoid irritants such as caffeine and alcohol.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care or emergency evaluation if fever develops, flank or testicular pain occurs, urinary retention, hematuria, or worsening symptoms. Follow-up with primary care or urology if symptoms persist beyond 7 days or recur frequently.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- sex_at_birth: male  
- fever: no  
- flank_pain: no  
- testicular_pain: no  
- med_allergies: none  
- Pregnancy not applicable
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
